I scraped this continuously, so I know what songs they played, how many times, and when. The Panama Papers revealed hidden bank accounts. This reveals hidden tastes.
# PANAMA PLAYLISTS
Published July 29, 2025.

I found the real Spotify accounts of celebrities, politicians, and journalists. Many use their real names. With a little investigating, I could say with near-certainty: yep, this is that person. I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year. Some individuals even have a setting enabled that displays their last played song. I scraped this continuously, so I know what songs they played

excerpts:
This paper outlines the methodology behind LooksMapping, a web-
based analysis that quantitatively evaluates restaurant clientele attrac-
tiveness, age, and gender ratios using neural network techniques.

Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

From these
accounts:
• Approximately 633,000 had no profile photo.
• Approximately 347,000 did not have a detectable face in their profile
photo.
• Approximately 587,000 had at least one detectable face.

Square profile photos were downloaded at 1,000-pixel resolution and processed
only if users had set a custom photo (excluding default Google avatars).

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-
related attributes. The phrases included:
• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.
citations.title: Methodology of LooksMapping: Quantifying Superficiality via ...

excerpts:
This paper outlines the methodology behind LooksMapping, a web-
based analysis that quantitatively evaluates restaurant clientele attrac-
tiveness, age, and gender ratios using neural network techniques.

Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-
related attributes. The phrases included:
• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.
Restaurants with fewer than 50 detected faces were excluded from the analysis.

# PANAMA PLAYLISTS
I found the real Spotify accounts of celebrities, politicians, and journalists. Many use their real names. With a little investigating, I could say with near-certainty: yep, this is that person.
I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year. Some individuals even have a setting enabled that displays their last played song. I scraped this continuously, so I know what songs they played, how many times, and when.
The Panama Papers revealed hidden bank accounts. This reveals hidden tastes.
Not affiliated with Spotify or any of the featured individuals. Album art and song previews from iTunes. Published July 29, 2025

# IMG_0001
Between 2009 and 2012, iPhones had a built-in "Send to YouTube" button in the Photos app. Many of these uploads kept their default IMG_XXXX filenames, creating a time capsule of raw, unedited moments from random lives.
Inspired by [Ben Wallace](https://ben-mini.github.io/2024/img-0416) , I made a bot that crawled YouTube and found 5 million of these videos! Watch them below, ordered randomly.

accounts:
• Approximately 633,000 had no profile photo.
• Approximately 347,000 did not have a detectable face in their profile
photo.
• Approximately 587,000 had at least one detectable face.

Square profile photos were downloaded at 1,000-pixel resolution and processed
only if users had set a custom photo (excluding default Google avatars).

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-
related attributes. The phrases included:
• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.
Restaurants with fewer than 50 detected faces were excluded from the analysis.

The CLIP model employed was the LAION-trained ”CLIP-ViT-B-32-laion2B-
s34B-b79K” from 2022 [1].

excerpts:
weather watching
LIVE
|AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention. **How this works →** |

walzr.com
THE WEATHER WATCHER
AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention.

A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ("You Only Look Once") watches the video feed continously, and draws a box around every pedestrian it sees. As people walk through the frame, it assigns each one a unique ID. It can follow people across multiple video frames, even if they briefly disappear behind someone else.
For each person, the system saves a single clear image to analyze what they're wearing.
Thanks to Brian for technical pointers, and James for the idea to do clothes in addition to umbrellas!
The answers get tallied across everyone who walked by. The crowd is producing a weather report for you.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

From these
accounts:
• Approximately 633,000 had no profile photo.
• Approximately 347,000 did not have a detectable face in their profile
photo.
• Approximately 587,000 had at least one detectable face.

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-

• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.
Restaurants with fewer than 50 detected faces were excluded from the analysis.

excerpts:
This paper outlines the methodology behind LooksMapping, a web-
based analysis that quantitatively evaluates restaurant clientele attrac-
tiveness, age, and gender ratios using neural network techniques.

Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

From these
accounts:
• Approximately 633,000 had no profile photo.
• Approximately 347,000 did not have a detectable face in their profile
photo.
• Approximately 587,000 had at least one detectable face.

Square profile photos were downloaded at 1,000-pixel resolution and processed
only if users had set a custom photo (excluding default Google avatars).

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-
related attributes. The phrases included:
• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.
Restaurants with fewer than 50 detected faces were excluded from the analysis.

excerpts:
This paper outlines the methodology behind LooksMapping, a web-
based analysis that quantitatively evaluates restaurant clientele attrac-
tiveness, age, and gender ratios using neural network techniques.

Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

From these
accounts:
• Approximately 633,000 had no profile photo.
• Approximately 347,000 did not have a detectable face in their profile
photo.
• Approximately 587,000 had at least one detectable face.

Square profile photos were downloaded at 1,000-pixel resolution and processed
only if users had set a custom photo (excluding default Google avatars).

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-
related attributes. The phrases included:
• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.
Restaurants with fewer than 50 detected faces were excluded from the analysis.

The CLIP model employed was the LAION-trained ”CLIP-ViT-B-32-laion2B-
s34B-b79K” from 2022 [1].
citations.title: Methodology of LooksMapping: Quantifying Superficiality via ...

# PANAMA PLAYLISTS
I found the real Spotify accounts of celebrities, politicians, and journalists. Many use their real names. With a little investigating, I could say with near-certainty: yep, this is that person.
I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year. Some individuals even have a setting enabled that displays their last played song. I scraped this continuously, so I know what songs they played, how many times, and when.
The Panama Papers revealed hidden bank accounts. This reveals hidden tastes.
Not affiliated with Spotify or any of the featured individuals. Album art and song previews from iTunes. Published July 29, 2025

This paper outlines the methodology behind LooksMapping, a web-
based analysis that quantitatively evaluates restaurant clientele attrac-
tiveness, age, and gender ratios using neural network techniques.

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.

Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.
Restaurants with fewer than 50 detected faces were excluded from the analysis.

excerpts:
This paper outlines the methodology behind LooksMapping, a web-
based analysis that quantitatively evaluates restaurant clientele attrac-
tiveness, age, and gender ratios using neural network techniques.

Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

. Each cropped facial image was scored against a pre-
defined set of descriptive phrases to determine attractiveness, age, and gender-
related attributes. The phrases included:
• ”She is attractive and beautiful”
• ”He is attractive and handsome”
• ”She is unattractive and ugly”
• ”He is unattractive and ugly”
• ”A young person”
• ”An old person”
CLI

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.

From these
accounts:
• Approximately 633,000 had no profile photo.
• Approximately 347,000 did not have a detectable face in their profile
photo.
• Approximately 587,000 had at least one detectable face.

Square profile photos were downloaded at 1,000-pixel resolution and processed
only if users had set a custom photo (excluding default Google avatars).

excerpts:
Data was collected from approximately 2.8 million Google Maps reviews across
9,834 restaurants located in New York, Los Angeles, and San Francisco.

Face detection was performed using the face recognition Python library, with
each photo cropped to the first detected face with a 30-pixel padding. In cases
where no face was detected, a center crop was performed.

Restaurant-level scores for attractiveness, age, and gender ratio were calcu-
lated by averaging individual normalized scores for each attribute, provided the
restaurant had at least 50 detected faces.

The CLIP model employed was the LAION-trained ”CLIP-ViT-B-32-laion2B-
s34B-b79K” from 2022 [1].
citations.title: Methodology of LooksMapping: Quantifying Superficiality via ...

weather watching

walzr.com
THE WEATHER WATCHER
AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention.
The answers get tallied across everyone who walked by. The crowd is producing a weather report for you.

A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ("You Only Look Once") watches the video feed continously, and draws a box around every pedestrian it sees. As people walk through the frame, it assigns each one a unique ID. It can follow people across multiple video frames, even if they briefly disappear behind someone else.
For each person, the system saves a single clear image to analyze what they're wearing.

excerpts:
weather watching
LIVE
|AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention. **How this works →** |

walzr.com
THE WEATHER WATCHER
AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention.
The answers get tallied across everyone who walked by. The crowd is producing a weather report for you.

A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ("You Only Look Once") watches the video feed continously, and draws a box around every pedestrian it sees. As people walk through the frame, it assigns each one a unique ID. It can follow people across multiple video frames, even if they briefly disappear behind someone else.
For each person, the system saves a single clear image to analyze what they're wearing.

excerpts:
weather watching
LIVE
|AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention. **How this works →** |

walzr.com
THE WEATHER WATCHER
AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention.
The answers get tallied across everyone who walked by. The crowd is producing a weather report for you.

A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ("You Only Look Once") watches the video feed continously, and draws a box around every pedestrian it sees. As people walk through the frame, it assigns each one a unique ID. It can follow people across multiple video frames, even if they briefly disappear behind someone else.
For each person, the system saves a single clear image to analyze what they're wearing.
citations.title: Weather Watching

# PANAMA PLAYLISTS
I found the real Spotify accounts of celebrities, politicians, and journalists. Many use their real names. With a little investigating, I could say with near-certainty: yep, this is that person.
I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year. Some individuals even have a setting enabled that displays their last played song. I scraped this continuously, so I know what songs they played, how many times, and when.
The Panama Papers revealed hidden bank accounts. This reveals hidden tastes.
Not affiliated with Spotify or any of the featured individuals. Album art and song previews from iTunes. Published July 29, 2025

content.executive_summary: An audit of Riley Walz's first-party project pages on walzr.com and related domains reveals him as a prolific data artist and creative technologist whose work exists at the intersection of playful prank, civic data analysis, and cultural critique. His creative process is defined by rapid prototyping and a 'publish-first, iterate-later' ethos, typically starting with a clever or provocative premise that is realized through the scraping or reverse-engineering of public and semi-public data sources. His aesthetic taste is consistently minimalist, favoring a 'data-forward, low-chrome' and text-first presentation that prioritizes the concept and the data over visual polish, aligning with a developer-centric or 'old internet' sensibility. These project pages collectively showcase a deep well of tacit knowledge, including practical skills in web scraping, API reverse-engineering, and interactive map-building, as well as a crucial, sophisticated ability to craft compelling and provocative narratives that effectively capture media attention and spark public discourse.
content.key_findings_synthesis:
The audit reveals several significant and recurring patterns in Riley Walz's work. His primary methodologies cluster around playful data acquisition and civic visualization. This includes:

*   **Playful Scraping and Reverse-Engineering:** A foundational technique is acquiring data through non-traditional means, such as reverse-engineering the SFMTA's parking ticket system for 'Find My Parking Cops', scraping millions of Google Maps reviews for 'Looksmapping', crawling YouTube for default-named videos in 'IMG_0001', and scanning Amazon for 'Postal Arbitrage'.
*   **Civic Data Visualization:** Many projects transform messy public or civic data into legible, interactive experiences, often using maps. Examples include 'Find My Parking Cops', the 'sf-911' call stream which visualizes data from data.sfgov.org, and 'Amtrak Right Now' which infers train occupancy.
*   **Ambient Sensing as Culture Probe:** A unique category of projects uses technology to sense the 'vibe' of a physical space, such as 'Bop Spotter' using a microphone and Shazam to log street music, or 'Weather Watching' using a camera and AI to analyze pedestrian clothing.

His aesthetic is consistently 'data-forward, low-chrome'. The visual design is intentionally minimalist to ensure the data and the concept are the primary focus. This is achieved through utilitarian UI choices like system fonts, monochrome color palettes where color is reserved for data emphasis (e.g., the 'red means hot, blue means not' in 'Looksmapping'), and simple, often single-column layouts. The projects are minimalist in UI but maximalist in their data ambition.

His approach to ethical considerations is informal and provocative. He often frames projects with self-aware, critical labels like 'culture surveillance' for 'Bop Spotter' and explicitly acknowledges limitations, such as stating 'The model is certainly biased' for 'Looksmapping'. He uses informal disclaimers ('Not affiliated with...') and candidly documents when his data sources are cut off. However, this candor is not accompanied by formal privacy policies, terms of service, or detailed ethical risk assessments, creating a notable gap between his reflective commentary and his formal practices.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: The project describes itself as a form of 'culture surveillance' that is 'not about catching criminals. It's about catching vibes.' It consists of a microphone placed in San Francisco's Mission district that uses Shazam 24/7 to identify music being played on the street. The stated goal is to surface the ambient, street-level musical culture of a neighborhood, playfully critiquing or mirroring the concept of city-wide audio surveillance like ShotSpotter.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: A physical microphone is pointed at a street, and its audio feed is continuously processed by Shazam to identify songs. The project involves hardware (microphone, likely with a small computer and solar power) and a third-party audio service ('Audio feed courtesy of Orchestra'). The results are collected and displayed in a live feed on the webpage.
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is playful, ironic, and provocative. The UI features a live, time-ordered table of detected songs with links to 'Open in Spotify / Open in Apple Music'. It also displays a running total of detections (e.g., 'Total Shazams ever detected: 7,885'). The copy is self-aware, using phrases like 'culture surveillance' and 'No one notices, no one consents.'
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The page explicitly credits 'Audio feed courtesy of Orchestra' and gives shoutouts to individuals (Drake, Nikhil, Raffi, Shihao) for help and to the art collective MSCHF for design inspiration. It frames itself with the provocative disclaimer 'This is culture surveillance.'
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: The project offers a full, machine-readable dataset. The page includes a link stating, 'Download a CSV of all Shazam results ever.' The direct URL is https://walzr.com/bop-spotter/export, which provides a CSV file containing columns for `shazam_id`, `time`, `title`, and `artist` for thousands of recorded detections.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: The project's goal was to provide transparency into municipal operations by showing the near real-time locations of San Francisco parking enforcement officers. It explains, 'I reverse engineered San Francisco's parking ticket system to show you exactly where each parking cop last wrote a ticket.' It frames the work as a civic data exposé, revealing a vulnerability in the city's system.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: The project involved reverse-engineering the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency's (SFMTA) parking ticket system. This was likely achieved by identifying a predictable pattern in citation IDs or scraping a public-facing payment portal to track officer activity. The data source was ephemeral and was cut off by the SFMTA after only four hours of operation.
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is investigative, revelatory, and slightly confrontational. The UI originally consisted of a live map and dashboard showing officer shorthand IDs, their last-known location, and a leaderboard. The current page is a static snapshot of that dashboard.
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The page contains a critical status message and disclaimer: 'This site only worked for 4 hours until the SFMTA cut the data source I was using. This data is a snapshot of how it looked on Sept. 25, 2025 at noon.' It also explicitly states it is 'Not affiliated with the San Francisco government or Apple's Find My.'
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: No data download is available. The project's output is preserved only as a static snapshot on the webpage, as the underlying data source was terminated.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: This project presents itself as a provocative social experiment and a critique of societal judgment. It states, 'I scraped millions of Google Maps restaurant reviews, and gave each reviewer's profile picture to an AI model that rates how hot they are out of 10.' The goal is to visualize and reflect on the superficial ways people judge places by their clientele, using AI as a mirror for these biases.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: The methodology involved scraping a large dataset of Google Maps restaurant reviews. The profile pictures of the reviewers were then processed using a face detection library (`face_recognition` in Python) and scored for attractiveness by a pre-trained AI model (LAION-trained 'CLIP-ViT-B-32-laion2B-s34B-b79K'). The scores were aggregated at the restaurant level and visualized on a map.
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is provocative and self-awarely critical. The UI is dominated by a map where restaurants are color-coded ('Red means hot, blue means not'). The copy directly confronts the project's flaws: 'The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed. But we judge places by the people who go there. We always have.'
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The page includes a 'Methodology • Made by Riley Walz' credit and attributes map data to '© OpenStreetMap'. The most significant disclaimer is the explicit and upfront admission of the AI model's inherent bias and flaws.
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: A formal methodology paper is available as a PDF at https://walzr.com/looksmapping/paper.pdf. This document provides a detailed breakdown of the data collection, image processing, modeling, and scoring rules. No raw data download is offered on the main page.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: The project is framed as an act of digital archeology and cultural preservation. It aims to surface 'raw, unedited moments from random lives' by collecting early iPhone videos uploaded to YouTube with their default 'IMG_XXXX' filenames. It functions as a time capsule of forgotten internet ephemera from the 2009-2012 era.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: A bot was created to crawl YouTube specifically searching for videos with default camera filenames like 'IMG_0001', 'IMG_0002', etc. The project claims to have found 5 million such videos, which are then presented to the user.
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is nostalgic, curious, and archival. The UI is a simple, minimalist video player interface that presents the discovered videos in a random order, encouraging exploration and serendipitous discovery.
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The project page gives credit for the concept, stating it was 'Inspired by Ben Wallace.'
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: There are no data downloads (like a list of video URLs or the scraper code) available on the project page. The output is the curated viewing experience itself.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: This project explains and exploits a pricing quirk in e-commerce. It demonstrates that one can buy and ship physical items via Amazon Prime for less than the cost of a standard US postage stamp. The framing is a playful question: 'Why send a postcard when you can send actual stuff?' It proposes this as a novel and cheaper alternative to traditional mail.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: The project continuously scrapes Amazon.com to find items that are available with free Prime shipping for a price lower than the current cost of a first-class stamp (e.g., under $0.78).
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is playful, tutorial-like, and persuasive, with a hint of a 'life hack' feel. The UI features a live-updating table of qualifying items and their prices, with a status message like 'Prices updated 0 minutes ago.' It includes a personal anecdote about using the method to send cans of beans to family.
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The page includes a clear and humorous disclaimer of non-affiliation: 'This site is not affiliated with or endorsed by Amazon, obviously!'
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: No data download is offered. The value of the project is the live, curated list of cheap items presented directly on the webpage.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: The project is an experiment in ambient sensing, described as an 'AI watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by.' It aims to quantify the 'weather' not by temperature, but by observing human behavior, specifically what people are wearing.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: The project uses a live video feed from a doorbell camera. A person-detection AI model called YOLO ('You Only Look Once') continuously identifies pedestrians. 'Best shot' frames of each person are then sent to Google's Gemini, a multimodal AI, for clothing classification (e.g., counting layers, umbrellas).
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is descriptive and technical, explaining the process clearly. The UI is a simple dashboard displaying live counts and percentages of observed clothing items. A link invites users to learn more: 'How this works →'.
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The page credits technical pointers received during the project's development.
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: No data download is mentioned on the project page.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: This project is framed as an act of investigative data journalism or leaking. It states, 'I found the real Spotify accounts of celebrities, politicians, and journalists... I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year.' The goal is to reveal the musical tastes of public figures by uncovering and publishing their personal playlists.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: The project involved investigating and identifying the personal Spotify accounts of public figures, likely through usernames and other public information. Once identified, their public playlists were scraped and collected over a long period.
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is that of a leak or an exposé. The UI presents lists of playlists and songs, often accompanied by the album art, organized by the public figure.
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The page includes a publication date: 'Published July 29, 2025.'
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: No data download is mentioned on the project page.

project_deep_dives.self_explanation: The project is a simple, personal utility. The creator explains, 'I'm Riley, a distance runner from New York. I made Routeshuffle to help with my summer training in high school.' Its goal is to solve a common problem for runners, walkers, and bikers: generating new and interesting routes of a specified distance from a starting point.
project_deep_dives.methodology_summary: Routeshuffle is a web-based tool that takes a starting location, a distance, and a route type (run, walk, or bike) as input and generates a random route loop. It likely uses a mapping API (like Google Maps or Mapbox) and its routing services to calculate the path.
project_deep_dives.ui_copy_and_tone: The tone is straightforward, personal, and helpful. The UI is a compact and simple web form where the user inputs their preferences and clicks 'Generate route'.
project_deep_dives.credits_and_disclaimers: The site includes a personal byline and origin story: 'I'm Riley... I made Routeshuffle...'
project_deep_dives.data_downloads: As a utility tool, it does not offer a data download.

creative_process_and_workflow.workflow_stages:
Riley Walz's creative process typically follows a pattern of rapid, concept-driven experimentation, often executed in a series of distinct stages. 
1. **Ideation and Framing:** Projects often begin with a playful, ironic, or provocative premise rooted in social commentary or data archeology. Themes include 'culture surveillance' (Bop Spotter), critiquing social judgment through AI (LooksMapping), exposing civic data quirks (Find My Parking Cops), and unearthing internet ephemera (IMG_0001). The initial idea is central and often framed to be compelling and shareable.
2. **Data Sourcing and Acquisition:** This is a core competency and a crucial early step. He employs a wide range of techniques, including extensive web scraping (e.g., Google Maps for LooksMapping, Amazon for Postal Arbitrage), reverse-engineering private or semi-public APIs (e.g., the SFMTA parking ticket system), utilizing public open data portals (e.g., data.sfgov.org for the 911 call stream), and crawling large platforms (e.g., a bot crawling YouTube for IMG_0001). In some cases, this stage involves deploying physical hardware, such as the microphone for Bop Spotter.
3. **Data Cleaning and Filtering:** While not always detailed, there is evidence of this stage. The 'LooksMapping' project, for instance, involved filtering out default avatars, enforcing minimum sample sizes for statistical validity, and handling cases where faces were not detected, demonstrating attention to data hygiene.
4. **Analysis and Modeling:** He tends to leverage existing, off-the-shelf tools rather than building models from scratch. For 'LooksMapping', he used a pre-trained CLIP model with custom prompts and scoring heuristics. For 'Weather Watching', he used the YOLO model for person detection. This approach prioritizes speed and accessibility.
5. **Visualization and Productization:** The output is almost always a public-facing, interactive web application. This can be a map (LooksMapping, sf-parking), a live feed (Bop Spotter), a searchable archive (Papers), or a randomized player (IMG_0001). The user interface is typically minimalist, focusing on presenting the data in a direct and engaging way. He also frequently provides raw data downloads, such as the CSV export for Bop Spotter.
6. **Build and Deployment:** The process is characterized by lean, agile, and rapid deployment. He appears to favor static site hosting and lightweight platforms like Glitch for Node.js applications, enabling him to launch projects quickly.
7. **Iteration and Maintenance:** Projects are treated as living experiments. This is evidenced by public TODO lists on GitHub (e.g., for 'musical-roulette'), frequently updated data indicators ('Prices updated 0 minutes ago' on 'Postal Arbitrage'), and candid notes about project status or data source interruptions.
creative_process_and_workflow.decision_heuristics:
Riley Walz's decision-making appears to be guided by a set of consistent rules of thumb that prioritize impact and speed over formal rigor. 
1. **Prioritize Speed and Prototyping over Code Perfection:** He explicitly favors shipping projects quickly to test an idea, even if the code is not pristine. The README for his 'dresden' project admits the code is "messy and probably isn't readable" because it was made quickly. This suggests a 'ship, observe, iterate' philosophy, where the value is in the public launch and reaction, not in the elegance of the initial codebase.
2. **Use Pre-trained, Accessible Tools:** For complex tasks like image analysis, he opts for well-documented, pre-trained models like CLIP and YOLO. He then builds custom scoring logic or prompts on top of them. This heuristic allows for rapid implementation of sophisticated features without the overhead of training bespoke models, making projects feasible for a solo developer.
3. **Embrace Provocative Framing:** Projects are often framed with a provocative or ironic tone (e.g., 'culture surveillance', rating people's attractiveness with AI). This is a deliberate choice to generate interest, spark conversation, and attract media attention, indicating a risk-tolerant approach that values visibility and social commentary.
4. **Value Data Novelty and Clever Sourcing:** The core of many projects is a novel dataset acquired through a clever method (scraping, reverse-engineering). The 'Find My Parking Cops' project, based on reverse-engineering predictable ticket IDs, is a prime example. This suggests he prioritizes the ingenuity of the data acquisition method as a key part of the project's value.
creative_process_and_workflow.iteration_habits:
Riley Walz's iteration habits are characterized by a public, responsive, and informal approach. 
1. **Public and Transparent Iteration:** He often develops in public. His GitHub repository for 'musical-roulette' includes a public TODO list with potential features like "let people pick if they want the sounds playing" and "add ability to rejoin the game." This signals an open development process where feedback and future plans are visible.
2. **Informal QA and Bug Fixing:** The evidence does not point to formal QA processes or CI/CD pipelines. Instead, quality assurance seems to be an ad-hoc process of testing, launching, and then fixing bugs as they are discovered or reported. The admission of "messy code" and the presence of public TODO lists support the idea of an iterative refinement process rather than a formal, pre-launch QA cycle.
3. **Responsiveness to User Feedback (Inferred):** The nature of the items in his public TODO lists (e.g., adding user-requested features for 'musical-roulette') suggests that he is open to and likely incorporates user feedback into his iteration cycle. The projects are not static releases but are subject to ongoing tweaks and improvements based on their reception and use.
4. **Maintaining Live Data Feeds:** For projects with live data, like 'Postal Arbitrage' ("Prices updated 0 minutes ago") and 'Bop Spotter' (a continuous feed of Shazamed songs), iteration involves ensuring the data pipelines remain active and the front-end continues to reflect the latest information.
creative_process_and_workflow.fallback_strategies:
Riley Walz demonstrates a pragmatic and transparent approach to handling project failures, particularly those dependent on fragile or ephemeral data sources. His main strategy is to treat experiments as time-bounded and to preserve their output for the record.
1. **Publishing Data Snapshots:** The most prominent example is the 'Find My Parking Cops' project. When the SFMTA cut off the data source after just four hours, he updated the page to explicitly state this and left the site live as a "snapshot of how it looked on Sept. 25, 2025 at noon." This strategy acknowledges the failure of the live project but preserves its output as a historical artifact and proof of concept.
2. **Acceptance of Ephemerality:** His process shows an inherent understanding that projects relying on scraping or reverse-engineering may have a short lifespan. Rather than seeing this as a total failure, he treats the short-lived experiment itself as a success, documenting its brief existence and impact. This reframes failure as a temporary success.
3. **Providing Raw Data Exports:** For projects like 'Bop Spotter', he offers a complete CSV download of "all Shazam results ever." This acts as a fallback strategy by creating a persistent, user-owned copy of the project's data. If the live feed were to ever go down, the collected data would still exist and be accessible, preserving the project's legacy.

technical_skills_and_toolchain.programming_languages_and_frameworks: Riley Walz's primary programming languages are JavaScript (for front-end interactivity and server-side logic) and Python (for data analysis and machine learning tasks). His GitHub profile shows a dominance of JavaScript projects, and the presence of `package.json` and `server.js` files in repositories like 'musical-roulette' and 'nypl-scrape' confirms heavy use of the Node.js runtime and ecosystem. Python is explicitly mentioned in the context of the 'LooksMapping' project, which used the `face_recognition` Python library.
technical_skills_and_toolchain.data_acquisition_techniques:
He demonstrates a mastery of multiple data acquisition techniques, which are often the centerpiece of his projects. These include:
- **Web Scraping:** He frequently scrapes websites for data, as seen in 'LooksMapping' (scraping millions of Google Maps reviews), 'Postal Arbitrage' (scraping Amazon for prices), and the 'nypl-scrape' GitHub repository.
- **Reverse-Engineering APIs:** A key skill, most notably demonstrated in the 'Find My Parking Cops' project, where he reverse-engineered the San Francisco parking ticket system, likely by identifying predictable patterns in citation IDs or undocumented API endpoints.
- **Using Public/Civic Datasets:** He leverages official open data portals, such as using the `data.sfgov.org` real-time 911 dataset for his 'sf-911' project.
- **Web Crawling:** For the 'IMG_0001' project, he created a bot that crawled YouTube at a large scale to find and catalog over 5 million videos with default filenames.
- **Consuming Third-Party Feeds:** The 'Bop Spotter' project integrates an audio feed from a service called Orchestra.
technical_skills_and_toolchain.analysis_and_ml_tooling:
For projects requiring machine learning, Walz typically employs powerful, pre-existing models and libraries rather than training his own from scratch. His toolset includes:
- **CLIP Model:** For the 'LooksMapping' project, he used the LAION-trained 'CLIP-ViT-B-32-laion2B-s34B-b79K' model to analyze and rate the attractiveness of profile pictures based on textual prompts.
- **Face Recognition Libraries:** The 'LooksMapping' methodology explicitly mentions using the `face_recognition` Python library to detect faces in scraped profile pictures.
- **YOLO (You Only Look Once):** The 'Weather Watching' project is described as using a YOLO model to perform real-time person detection on a video feed.
- **Large Multimodal Models:** For 'Weather Watching', after detecting people with YOLO, he sends 'best shot' frames to Google's Gemini, a multimodal AI, for more complex image interpretation like clothing classification.
technical_skills_and_toolchain.hosting_and_deployment:
His hosting and deployment strategy appears to favor lightweight, fast, and often free or low-cost solutions that enable rapid prototyping. The evidence points to:
- **Static Site Hosting:** The general structure and performance of walzr.com and its sub-pages suggest deployment on a modern static site hosting platform like Vercel, Netlify, or GitHub Pages.
- **Glitch:** The README for his 'dresden' project explicitly acknowledges using Glitch for its free Node.js hosting capabilities, indicating a preference for serverless or containerized platforms that are easy to set up for backend logic.
- **Mapping Stacks:** For geospatial projects, he integrates with mapping providers like OpenStreetMap and Protomaps, deploying interactive maps within his web applications.
technical_skills_and_toolchain.third_party_services:
His projects are often clever integrations of various third-party services, combining them in novel ways. Key services include:
- **Shazam:** The core technology behind the 'Bop Spotter' project, used to identify music from a live audio feed.
- **Orchestra:** Explicitly credited as the provider of the live audio feed for 'Bop Spotter'.
- **OpenStreetMap:** His preferred source for base map data, as credited in 'LooksMapping' and other mapping projects.
- **Google (Maps, Gemini, YouTube):** He has scraped Google Maps for data ('LooksMapping'), crawled YouTube for video content ('IMG_0001'), and used the Gemini model for AI analysis ('Weather Watching').
- **Amazon:** The 'Postal Arbitrage' project is based on scraping product and pricing data from Amazon.

aesthetic_and_design_taste.overall_style: A 'data-forward, text-first, low-chrome' aesthetic that prioritizes speed, clarity, and the core concept over visual polish. The style is minimalist in its visual treatment (simple typography, sparse CSS) but often maximalist in its data ambition (large scraped datasets, live streams). It aligns with a developer-centric or 'old internet' sensibility, where the idea and the data are the main attraction, not the UI design.
aesthetic_and_design_taste.typography_and_color: Typography is plain, utilitarian, and text-forward, with a clear preference for system UI fonts over custom web fonts to ensure fast performance. Color palettes are predominantly monochrome (black/gray text on white backgrounds), with vivid colors used sparingly and purposefully to highlight data visualizations, such as the red ('hot') and blue ('not') overlays in the Looksmapping project.
aesthetic_and_design_taste.layout_and_interaction: Layouts are typically single-column and text-first, frequently using simple tables and lists to present dense data (e.g., the song feed in Bop Spotter). Interaction is functional and data-driven, featuring live updates, simple forms, and direct links to data (e.g., 'Open in Spotify'). Decorative animations are minimal, with real-time behavior implemented solely for data freshness.
aesthetic_and_design_taste.data_visualization_style: Preferred methods are direct and unadorned, including tiled maps (often using OpenStreetMap), heat/score overlays, simple time-ordered tables and lists, and basic numeric dashboards. A key feature is the frequent offering of raw data downloads, typically as a CSV file, which emphasizes data transparency over complex, embedded charting libraries.

communication_style_and_tone.overall_tone: The tone is consistently playful, irreverent, self-aware, and often provocative or confrontational. The writing is punchy, witty, and informal, framing projects as playful interventions, cultural pranks, or social critiques. There is a strong authorial voice that is both humorous and critical, especially when dealing with themes of surveillance, data privacy, and platform absurdities.
communication_style_and_tone.rhetorical_devices: Common techniques include short, punchy headlines and taglines; a strong first-person narrative ('I made a bot', 'I'm Riley'); framing projects with provocative analogies ('culture surveillance'); using social shoutouts to credit collaborators and inspirations; and writing 'defensively cheeky' methodology notes that candidly acknowledge biases or limitations ('The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed.').
communication_style_and_tone.microcopy_examples: Examples include direct calls to action like 'Download a CSV of all Shazam results ever' and 'Read how I'm getting this data'; status updates such as 'Prices updated 0 minutes ago' and the ephemeral note 'This site only worked for 4 hours...'; and explanatory labels that also set the tone, like 'Red means hot, blue means not' and 'This is culture surveillance.'

cross_cutting_themes_and_project_cluster.major_themes:
Riley Walz's work is characterized by several recurring cross-cutting themes that appear across his portfolio:

1.  **Playful Data & Internet Archeology:** A significant portion of his work involves unearthing, curating, and re-presenting forgotten or overlooked digital artifacts. This is evident in projects like 'IMG_0001', which involved creating a bot to find and display 5 million early, default-named iPhone video uploads to YouTube, framing them as a "time-capsule." Another example is 'Papers', a daily archive of newspaper front pages. This theme treats the internet as a site for playful, curiosity-driven exploration and archival.

2.  **Ambient Surveillance as a Culture Probe:** Walz frequently uses the language and tools of surveillance not for control, but as a method for cultural observation and commentary. The 'Bop Spotter' project is a prime example, described as a microphone on a San Francisco street that constantly Shazams music. The project page explicitly calls this "culture surveillance," stating, "No one notices, no one consents. But it's not about catching criminals. It's about catching vibes." Similarly, 'Weather Watching' uses an AI-powered camera to count pedestrians and analyze their clothing, turning public space into a passive sensor for social trends.

3.  **Mapping and Civic-Data Visualization:** A major theme is the use of maps and data visualization to make invisible civic and urban systems legible, often with a provocative or critical edge. Projects like 'Find My Parking Cops' reverse-engineered the SFMTA's system to map the real-time locations of parking enforcement officers. 'Looksmapping' scraped Google Maps reviews and used AI to rate reviewers' attractiveness, then mapped the average "hotness" of each restaurant's clientele. 'Amtrak Right Now' infers and displays seat inventory on trains. These projects take public or semi-public data and present it in a way that reveals patterns and raises questions about urban life and governance.
cross_cutting_themes_and_project_cluster.project_clusters:
Based on their core methods and outputs, Riley Walz's projects can be categorized into several distinct clusters:

1.  **Archival/Randomization:** These projects focus on collecting and presenting large datasets of cultural ephemera. 
    *   **Examples:** 'IMG_0001' (randomized feed of old YouTube videos), 'Papers' (archive of newspaper front pages).

2.  **Ambient Sensing / Culture Surveillance:** These projects deploy physical or digital sensors to capture and analyze ambient data from public spaces.
    *   **Examples:** 'Bop Spotter' (live Shazam feed from a street microphone), 'Weather Watching' (AI camera analyzing pedestrian clothing).

3.  **Mapping & Civic Data:** This cluster includes projects that use geospatial data and mapping to visualize and critique civic or social phenomena.
    *   **Examples:** 'Looksmapping' (mapping restaurant clientele attractiveness), 'Find My Parking Cops' (mapping parking officer activity), 'Amtrak Right Now' (mapping train inventory), 'SF 911' (mapping real-time 911 calls).

4.  **Generators/Playful Utilities:** These are often smaller, single-purpose tools or websites that generate content or exploit a particular system quirk.
    *   **Examples:** 'Routeshuffle' (random route generator for runners), 'Postal Arbitrage' (finding Amazon items cheaper than a postage stamp), 'HDR2' (a since-removed playful page).

data_handling_and_reproducibility_practi.source_crediting: Riley Walz's approach to source crediting is consistent but informal. He typically provides credit in plain English within the project's description rather than in a formal, structured format. Examples are abundant across his work: 'Bop Spotter' credits its "Audio feed courtesy of Orchestra" and gives shoutouts to individuals and MSCHF for inspiration; 'LooksMapping' credits "Map data © OpenStreetMap"; and 'IMG_0001' credits Ben Wallace for the inspiration. This practice demonstrates a clear intent to acknowledge sources and collaborators, aligning with open-source community norms, though it lacks the machine-readability of formal metadata.
data_handling_and_reproducibility_practi.licensing_approach: There is a significant and consistent gap in Riley Walz's licensing practices. Across the audited project pages and sampled GitHub repositories, there is a near-total absence of explicit licenses for either his source code or his datasets. No `LICENSE` files were found in his GitHub repos, and no terms of use or creative commons licenses were displayed on his project pages. In most legal jurisdictions, the absence of a license defaults to exclusive copyright ("all rights reserved"), which legally prohibits others from using, modifying, or distributing the work. This creates significant legal ambiguity and risk for anyone wishing to build upon or reproduce his projects, and it stands in contrast to common practices in the open-data and open-source communities.
data_handling_and_reproducibility_practi.data_availability_and_formats: His practice regarding data availability is mixed. On one hand, he demonstrates a clear commitment to data openness in some projects. For 'Bop Spotter', he provides a prominent link to "Download a CSV of all Shazam results ever," making the project's entire raw output available in a simple, machine-readable format. On the other hand, for many other projects like 'LooksMapping' and 'IMG_0001', he publishes a derived visualization (a map, a video feed) but does not provide a direct download link for the underlying scraped data or analysis results. In the case of the defunct 'sf-parking' project, he made a final data snapshot available, indicating a practice of preserving data even when a project ends.
data_handling_and_reproducibility_practi.reproducibility_signals:
The reproducibility of his projects is highly variable, with a mix of positive and negative signals. 
**Positive Signals:** The availability of public GitHub repositories with source code, the publication of raw data via CSV downloads ('Bop Spotter'), and high-level methodology descriptions on project pages all contribute to reproducibility.
**Negative Signals:** The lack of explicit licenses is a major barrier. Furthermore, many projects rely on reverse-engineered, ephemeral APIs that are not documented and can be shut down, as was the case with 'Find My Parking Cops'. While he may link to a 'Methodology' page, some of these links are broken (404 errors). Crucial details for reproduction, such as specific scraping parameters, data schemas for CSV files, and step-by-step runbooks, are often missing. Therefore, while one could be inspired by his methods, a third party would find it difficult or impossible to precisely replicate many of his projects from the provided information alone.

ethical_and_risk_analysis.ethical_framing: Riley Walz often frames the ethical implications of his work directly on the project pages, using a tone that is both candid and provocative. Rather than avoiding difficult topics, he leans into them. For 'Bop Spotter', he explicitly labels the project "culture surveillance," co-opting a loaded term to describe its function of capturing ambient street music, and adds, "No one notices, no one consents." For 'LooksMapping', a project that uses AI to rate the attractiveness of people's profile pictures, he directly confronts the inherent issues by stating, "The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed... This website just puts reductive numbers on the superficial calculations we make every day." This approach serves as a form of social commentary, using the project itself as a mirror to reflect on societal behaviors and the flaws of technology, rather than presenting the technology as neutral or objective.
ethical_and_risk_analysis.disclaimers_and_caveats:
Informal disclaimers and caveats are a consistent feature across his projects, used to manage expectations and clarify affiliations. These are typically short, plain-language notes integrated into the project descriptions.

*   **Non-Affiliation:** Projects that interact with corporate or government entities often include explicit disclaimers. For example, 'Postal Arbitrage' states, "This site is not affiliated with or endorsed by Amazon, obviously!" and 'Find My Parking Cops' notes it is "Not affiliated with the San Francisco government or Apple's Find My."
*   **Data Source Limitations:** He is transparent about the fragility and limitations of his data sources. The 'Find My Parking Cops' page famously includes the banner: "This site only worked for 4 hours until the SFMTA cut the data source I was using. This data is a snapshot of how it looked on Sept. 25, 2025 at noon." This serves as both a disclaimer and a narrative element of the project's lifecycle.
*   **Methodological Caveats:** Projects like 'Amtrak Right Now' include notes on potential inaccuracies, stating that counts "may undercount unreserved tickets, pass riders, or routes where Amtrak does not expose full seat inventory."
ethical_and_risk_analysis.privacy_mitigations: While many projects operate in a gray area, some demonstrate explicit, albeit simple, privacy mitigation techniques. The 'SF 911' project, which displays a near real-time feed of 911 calls from a public dataset, includes a privacy note explaining the steps taken to reduce harm. The page states that for privacy, locations are generalized to the "closest intersection" rather than showing precise addresses. Furthermore, it notes that "some sensitive calls are redacted" from the public feed he is using, acknowledging and passing on the privacy measures implemented by the data provider (data.sfgov.org). These examples show an awareness of privacy risks and the implementation of basic harm-reduction strategies like data generalization and acknowledging redaction.
ethical_and_risk_analysis.identified_risks:
The nature of Riley Walz's work entails several potential risks, as identified from the project descriptions and methodologies:

*   **Legal and Terms of Service (ToS) Risks:** Many projects rely on web scraping or reverse-engineering, which can violate the ToS of platforms like Google Maps ('Looksmapping'), YouTube ('IMG_0001'), or municipal systems ('Find My Parking Cops'). The rapid shutdown of the 'Find My Parking Cops' data source by the SFMTA is a concrete example of this risk materializing.
*   **Privacy Risks:** Projects that collect and publish data about individuals without their explicit consent pose significant privacy risks. 'Looksmapping' scraped and rated profile pictures, and 'Find My Parking Cops' published the recent locations of government employees. While framed as art or commentary, these actions involve processing personal data in ways individuals would not expect.
*   **Safety Risks:** The 'Find My Parking Cops' project, by tracking the locations of parking officers, could have potentially created safety risks for those individuals, a concern noted by city officials in press coverage.
*   **Reproducibility and Reuse Risks:** The general lack of explicit open-source or data licenses on his projects and datasets creates legal ambiguity for others who might wish to build upon or reuse his work. Without a clear license, the default is often "all rights reserved," which contradicts the open-data ethos some of his projects seem to embrace.

content.summary_of_tacit_knowledge:
Riley Walz's project pages reveal a significant amount of tacit, practical knowledge that underpins his creative output. This implicit knowledge goes beyond the explicit project descriptions and is demonstrated through the consistent execution and impact of his work:

*   **Web Scraping and Reverse-Engineering:** He demonstrates a mastery of acquiring data from sources not intended for automated access. This is evident in 'Find My Parking Cops' (reverse-engineering a municipal ticketing system), 'Postal Arbitrage' (scraping Amazon), 'Looksmapping' (scraping Google Maps), and the 'nypl-scrape' GitHub repository. This skill includes identifying predictable patterns in APIs and web UIs to extract data at scale.

*   **Working with Messy Civic Datasets:** Projects like 'sf-911' and 'Find My Parking Cops' show an ability to work with real-world government data, which is often incomplete, ephemeral, or requires cleaning and privacy-preserving transformations (e.g., fuzzing locations to the nearest intersection).

*   **Building Interactive Map UIs:** A recurring skill is the ability to present geospatial data effectively. He demonstrates proficiency with mapping libraries and services like OpenStreetMap and Protomaps to create interactive visualizations for projects like 'Looksmapping', 'Amtrak Right Now', and 'Find My Parking Cops'.

*   **Crafting Provocative Project Framing to Attract Media Attention:** Perhaps his most crucial non-technical skill is his ability to frame his projects with short, witty, and provocative narratives. Describing 'Bop Spotter' as 'culture surveillance' or framing 'Looksmapping' as a mirror to societal vanity are highly effective communication strategies. This skill in crafting a compelling story is directly responsible for the media attention his projects (like 'Find My Parking Cops', which was covered by the NYT and SFist) receive, turning technical experiments into public conversations.

*   **Rapid Prototyping and Deployment:** The ephemeral nature and rapid succession of his projects indicate a strong competency in using lightweight technology stacks (e.g., JavaScript/Node.js, static site hosting) to quickly build and deploy functional web applications.

*   **Data Publication and Openness:** The consistent practice of offering raw data downloads, such as the CSV export for 'Bop Spotter', demonstrates a tacit understanding of open data principles and an implicit knowledge of how to structure and serve data for public consumption.

inferred_likes_and_dislikes.inferred_likes:
Based on the consistent choices in his projects, Riley Walz appears to favor:

*   **Urban Data and Hidden Systems:** A clear fascination with the data trails of city life, from music on the street ('Bop Spotter') to parking enforcement patterns ('Find My Parking Cops') and 911 calls ('SF 911').
*   **Playful Subversion and Pranks:** A recurring enjoyment in finding and exploiting loopholes or absurdities in systems, such as in 'Postal Arbitrage' (sending items cheaper than a stamp) or 'Find My Parking Cops' (exposing a predictable system).
*   **Raw Data and Transparency:** A preference for making underlying data available. This is most evident in 'Bop Spotter', which offers a complete CSV download of all detected songs, and 'SF 911', which links directly to the source open data portal.
*   **Internet Archeology:** An appreciation for the ephemeral and forgotten corners of the internet, as seen in the 'IMG_0001' project, which salvages old, unlisted YouTube videos.
*   **Minimalist, 'Low-Chrome' Aesthetics:** A design taste that prioritizes function, speed, and data clarity over visual polish. His UIs are often text-forward, use system fonts, and feature simple layouts, echoing an 'old internet' or developer-centric style.
*   **Provocation as Commentary:** Using provocative concepts ('Looksmapping', 'culture surveillance') to spark conversation and critique social norms and technological biases.
inferred_likes_and_dislikes.inferred_dislikes:
Conversely, his work implicitly critiques or shows a dislike for:

*   **Corporate UI Polish and Over-Design:** His consistent use of minimalist, utilitarian design suggests a rejection of the heavy, over-stylized interfaces common in mainstream tech products. His work is the antithesis of a polished corporate design system.
*   **Platform Opacity and 'Black Boxes':** Many of his projects aim to reveal the inner workings of opaque systems, whether it's a municipal ticketing API ('Find My Parking Cops') or the cultural zeitgeist ('Bop Spotter'). This implies a dislike for systems that hide their logic from users.
*   **'Tech Theatre' and Uncritical Hype:** By candidly acknowledging the flaws and biases in his own AI projects ('Looksmapping'), he shows a distaste for the uncritical hype that often surrounds technologies like AI. His work often serves to demystify or satirize tech.
*   **Ephemeral, Unarchived Culture:** Projects like 'IMG_0001' and 'Papers' suggest a dislike for the transient nature of digital content and a desire to archive and preserve it.

temporal_evolution_of_work.early_phase: Riley Walz's earlier work appears to be focused on simpler web-based generators, archival projects, and playful utilities. Projects from this phase are often single-purpose and demonstrate a foundational interest in web scraping and internet culture. Examples include 'Routeshuffle', a simple random route generator for runners created during his high school years, and 'IMG_0001', a project dedicated to crawling and archiving millions of old, default-named videos from YouTube. This phase is characterized by a focus on found internet content and the creation of lightweight, often nostalgic, digital toys.
temporal_evolution_of_work.middle_phase: This phase marks a distinct shift towards more complex projects involving civic data, reverse-engineering, and mapped visualizations. The work becomes more engaged with real-world systems and urban environments. The 'Find My Parking Cops' project, which gained media attention around 2025, is a hallmark of this period. It involved reverse-engineering a municipal API to create a live map of parking officer activity. Other projects like 'Amtrak Right Now', which infers and displays train seat inventory, also fit into this phase, demonstrating an increasing sophistication in scraping and presenting data from civic or corporate infrastructure.
temporal_evolution_of_work.recent_phase: His more recent work shows a further evolution, incorporating contemporary AI tools for social commentary and expanding into physical hardware experiments. 'Looksmapping' is a key example of this phase, using a pre-trained AI model (CLIP) to rate profile pictures and generate a provocative map about social judgment. This demonstrates a move from simply presenting data to using complex models to interpret it. The 'Bop Spotter' project marks another significant development: the deployment of physical hardware. By setting up a solar-powered microphone on a city street, he expanded his practice beyond purely digital scraping and into the realm of instrumenting the physical world, blending his interests in urban data and ambient culture.
temporal_evolution_of_work.key_inflection_points:
Several key projects and technological shifts mark significant turning points in his work:

1.  **The Move into Mapping and Civic Data:** The creation of projects like 'Find My Parking Cops' represents a major inflection point, moving from playful web toys to direct engagement with and exposure of real-world municipal systems. This pivot brought his work into the realm of civic tech and data journalism.
2.  **The Incorporation of AI for Social Commentary:** The 'Looksmapping' project marks the point where he began using sophisticated, off-the-shelf AI models not just as a tool, but as the subject of critique itself. This added a new layer of sociocultural commentary to his work, engaging with contemporary debates about AI bias and ethics.
3.  **The Deployment of Physical Hardware:** 'Bop Spotter' signifies a crucial expansion of his toolkit beyond the web. By building and deploying a physical, internet-connected sensor in an urban environment, he bridged the gap between his digital practice and the physical world, opening up new avenues for 'ambient sensing' projects.

notable_project_excerpts.excerpt: Somewhere in the Mission district of San Francisco is a microphone, pointed down at the street below. It is using Shazam constantly, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. This is culture surveillance. No one notices, no one consents. But it's not about catching criminals. It's about catching vibes.
Total Shazams ever detected: 7,885. Download a CSV of all Shazam results ever.
I reverse engineered San Francisco's parking ticket system to show you exactly where each parking cop last wrote a ticket. Updates in real-time. Read how I'm getting this data.
This site only worked for 4 hours until the SFMTA cut the data source I was using. This data is a snapshot of how it looked on Sept. 25, 2025 at noon.

I scraped millions of Google Maps restaurant reviews, and gave each reviewer's profile picture to an AI model that rates how hot they are out of 10. This map shows how attractive each restaurant's clientele is. Red means hot, blue means not.
The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed. But we judge places by the people who go there. We always have.

Between 2009 and 2012, iPhones had a built-in 'Send to YouTube' button in the Photos app. ... Inspired by Ben Wallace, I made a bot that crawled YouTube and found 5 million of these videos! Watch them below, ordered randomly.

As of 2025, a stamp for a letter costs $0.78 in the United States. Amazon Prime sells items for less than that... with free shipping! ... This site is not affiliated with or endorsed by Amazon, obviously!

AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. ... A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ('You Only Look Once') watches the video feed continuously...
I'm Riley, a distance runner from New York. I made Routeshuffle to help with my summer training in high school.

I found the real Spotify accounts of celebrities, politicians, and journalists. Many use their real names. With a little investigating, I could say with near-certainty: yep, this is that person. I’ve been scraping their playlists for over a year… Published July 29, 2025.
You are logged in as Jeffrey Epstein. These are real emails released by Congress. Explore by name, search, visit a random page , contribute to the starred list, or vote for an unredaction. ?

content.identified_gaps_and_weaknesses:
Despite the creativity and technical skill demonstrated, the audit of Riley Walz's first-party projects reveals several consistent gaps and weaknesses in his practices, primarily concerning legal clarity, reproducibility, and project longevity.

*   **Consistent Lack of Explicit Code/Data Licenses:** This is the most significant and pervasive weakness. Across his project pages and public GitHub repositories, there is a near-total absence of `LICENSE` files or explicit statements defining terms of use for his code or the datasets he publishes. In the absence of a license, the default is 'all rights reserved,' which creates legal ambiguity and friction for others wishing to learn from, reuse, or build upon his work. This practice is at odds with the open, playful spirit many of his projects seem to embody.

*   **Missing Detailed and Reproducible Methodology:** While Walz provides high-level, narrative explanations for his projects, detailed, step-by-step methodologies are often missing. Links to 'Methodology' pages sometimes result in 404 errors (as seen with 'Looksmapping'), and the reverse-engineering techniques or specific scraping scripts are rarely published. This lack of detailed documentation makes it difficult for others to independently verify his findings or replicate his experiments, limiting their value for academic or journalistic purposes.

*   **Ephemeral Nature of Experiments:** Many of his most interesting projects are, by his own admission, ephemeral. 'Find My Parking Cops' worked for only four hours before its data source was cut off. While he commendably preserves snapshots of these projects, their transient nature limits their long-term utility and impact. This practice, while enabling rapid experimentation, results in a portfolio of often-defunct projects rather than durable tools or resources.

*   **Informal and Incomplete Ethical Frameworks:** While he is candid about the ethical provocations of his work, his approach lacks formal rigor. There are no formal privacy policies, terms of service, or detailed ethical risk assessments, even for projects that scrape personal data ('Looksmapping') or track individuals ('Find My Parking Cops'). This exposes him to potential legal and ethical risks and relies on informal disclaimers rather than robust, transparent policies.

inferred_dislikes.description: A list of topics, concepts, or styles he appears to dislike or critique, such as corporate UI polish, platform opacity, and over-designed systems.

middle_phase.description: Description of the shift towards mapped civic data, scraping civic records, and more complex visualizations.

recent_phase.description: Description of his more recent work incorporating AI for social commentary and deploying physical hardware experiments.
